model_narrative: We plan electricity capacity expansion under uncertainty about future demand. First we decide how much generation capacity to install for each type of power plant, before we know which future scenario will occur. Then, once a scenario is revealed, we decide how much each plant type produces in each operating mode to serve that scenario's demand. We track the total cost, which combines the upfront investment cost and the expected operating cost across the scenarios. The objective is to make the total cost as small as possible.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the types of power plant whose capacity can be installed

sets.name: j
sets.doc: the operating modes in which installed plants can run

sets.name: s
sets.doc: the future scenarios for second-stage operation, each with its own probability

params.name: c
params.doc: investment cost to install one unit of capacity of each plant type, in cost units per unit of capacity

params.name: d
params.doc: the baseline energy demand of each operating mode, in energy units

params.name: f
params.doc: operating cost to run a plant type in an operating mode, in cost units per unit of energy produced

params.name: m
params.doc: the minimum total installed capacity that must be in place across all plant types, in units of capacity

params.name: b
params.doc: the budget limit available for capacity investment, in cost units

params.name: dvar
params.doc: the scenario-dependent energy demand for the first operating mode, in energy units, which differs from one scenario to the next

params.name: prob
params.doc: the probability of each scenario, summing to one across all scenarios

params.name: ds
params.doc: the energy demand of each operating mode under each scenario, in energy units; for the first operating mode it is the scenario-dependent demand and for every other mode it is the baseline demand of that mode

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the amount of capacity installed for each plant type, a first-stage decision made before the scenario is known

vars.name: ys
vars.doc: the operating level of each plant type in each operating mode under each scenario, a second-stage recourse decision made after the scenario is revealed, in energy units

vars.name: cost
vars.doc: the total cost of the plan, combining first-stage investment cost and expected second-stage operating cost

constraints.name: x[i]
constraints.intent: The total capacity installed across all plant types must reach at least the required minimum total capacity. Add up the capacity installed for every plant type and make sure it is no less than that minimum.

constraints.name: c[i]
constraints.intent: Investment spending cannot exceed the available budget. Add up the investment cost of the capacity installed for every plant type and make sure the total stays within the budget limit.

constraints.name: cost
constraints.intent: The total cost combines the upfront investment and the expected operating cost. Add the investment cost of the capacity installed across all plant types to the operating cost of running every plant type in every mode under every scenario, with each scenario's operating cost weighted by how likely that scenario is, and set the total cost equal to that sum.

constraints.name: ys[i,
constraints.intent: No plant type can produce more than its installed capacity in any scenario. For each plant type and each scenario, the total amount it produces across all operating modes must not exceed the capacity installed for that plant type.

constraints.name: ys[i,
constraints.intent: Demand must be met in every operating mode under every scenario. For each operating mode and each scenario, the total amount produced across all plant types must be at least that mode's demand in that scenario.

query: We're looking at a low-cost-build mandate: the two cheapest units to install - plant-4 and plant-2 - should together carry at least 8 units of installed capacity, so the portfolio leans on the assets that are cheap to stand up. How does that combined floor on the low-cost pair move the build mix and expected total cost?

query: The plan pours capacity into plant-3, our priciest unit to install, while plant-4 - the cheapest to build - barely gets off the ground. Shouldn't the cheapest-to-install plant always carry at least one unit more capacity than the most expensive one, so the build leans on the bargain hardware? What's the tradeoff the model is seeing that keeps it from making that move?
